target.title: fix(devices): always route stale-approve operator to openclaw devices list
target.body:
> AI-assisted: drafted with Claude (Opus 4.7). Bug surfaced by the agent
> while operating an OpenClaw 2026.5.7 ECS Fargate deployment in production;
> full transcript available on request.

> **Update (rebased 2026-05-14):** Post-refactor in `fe25ed214e` ("refactor(cli): lazy-load devices runtime"), the
> approve fallback code moved from `src/cli/devices-cli.ts` to `src/cli/devices-cli.runtime.ts`. This PR's fix
> now applies in the new file; the bug (`return null` silent path → bare `unknown requestId` caller log) is
> still present on main and the diff/tests remain the same shape, just at the new file location.

## Summary

`openclaw devices approve <requestId>` on a loopback gateway has a local
file fallback path (`src/cli/devices-cli.runtime.ts:220-271` (post-refactor; was `src/cli/devices-cli.ts:280-358`)) that approves
directly against `~/.openclaw/devices/{pending.json,paired.json}` when
the CLI cannot dial the gateway as an admin. The fallback compares the
user-supplied `requestId` against `requestId` extracted from the
gateway's pairing-required close error. Three branches today:

1. Gateway error names a different requestId → throw
   `FALLBACK_STATE_MISMATCH_MESSAGE` with `Missing requestId: <gatewayId>`.
   Actionable. ✅
2. Gateway error names the same requestId, but local pending lacks it
   → throw the same state-mismatch error. Actionable. ✅
3. Gateway error names **no** requestId, and local pending lacks the
   user-supplied id → silent `return null`. The caller at the caller line in the runtime file
   only logs `defaultRuntime.error("unknown requestId")`. ❌

Branch 3 fires whenever the gateway emits a `not-paired` close
(`buildPairingConnectErrorMessage` in
`src/gateway/protocol/connect-error-details.ts:275-281` returns the
bare `"pairing required"` string without a parenthetical) **and** the
user-supplied requestId is no longer in `pending.json`. The most common
reason for that disappearance is *supersession*: the gateway dial just
called `requestDevicePairing` → `reconcilePendingPairingRequests` →
`canRefreshSingle` returned false because the incoming and existing
scope sets diverge (`samePendingApprovalSnapshot` requires exact set
equality, `src/infra/device-pairing.ts:314-331`), so the reconciler
**replaced** the entry with a fresh `requestId`. Other causes: the 5
minute TTL (`PENDING_TTL_MS` at `src/infra/device-pairing.ts:132`) or
running approve under a different `OPENCLAW_PROFILE`/`OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`.

The operator is left with `unknown requestId` and no pointer to the
current pending state or what to do next.

## Reproduction (before)

ECS Fargate container running `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:2026.5.7`,
Linux x86_64, loopback gateway, paired operator device with only
`operator.read`. Generated a pending request via
`openclaw devices approve --latest` preview (which builds a
scope-upgrade request for the full `CLI_DEFAULT_OPERATOR_SCOPES` set),
then ran the explicit approve:

```text
$ openclaw devices approve 11e7be5a-a243-4855-9439-ccc9291449ba --json
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: scope upgrade pending approval (requestId: 1dce2124-3c2d-4fa6-98f9-77ccf1e422d1)
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: scope upgrade pending approval (requestId: 34a9feb9-847c-48d7-8a42-057c4d3dab82)
unknown requestId
```

`11e7be5a` was the user-supplied id from the preview. The dial built a
new pending whose scope set was a narrower subset of the user-supplied
one (just `operator.pairing` to call `device.pair.approve`); the
reconciler replaced rather than refreshed and `11e7be5a` was gone by
the time the fallback ran. The operator has no way to discover from
this output that the current pending entry exists locally.

## Fix

Replace the silent `return null` with a thrown `Error` whose message
includes:

- the user-supplied (stale) requestId
- a one-line list of plausible causes (supersession during dial, TTL
  prune, profile mismatch)
- a count of other pending entries visible in this profile
- a routing line: `openclaw devices list`

The diagnostic **never prints a direct `Rerun: openclaw devices approve <id>`
command**, even when only one other pending entry is visible. Three
plausible causes (TTL prune, profile/state mismatch, multi-device
profile churn) can each produce a single visible pending that is
unrelated to the operator's intent. The earlier ClawSweeper review
(`P2: Single pending request can be unrelated`) called this out
explicitly. Routing through `openclaw devices list` forces the
operator to verify deviceId + requested scopes before approving,
matching the existing implicit-approve `--latest` flow which is also
preview-only.

No behavior change to the approval semantics in any branch. The other
two error paths (mismatched gatewayRequestId, gatewayRequestId matches
but pending missing) keep the existing `buildFallbackStateMismatchError`
text; only the previously silent `return null` path now throws an
actionable error.

## After

### No other pending entries

```text
$ openclaw devices approve req-stale --json
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "req-stale" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
No other pending requests are visible in this profile. List pending requests with: openclaw devices list
```

### One other pending entry (still routes to `list` — could be unrelated)

```text
$ openclaw devices approve 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111 --json
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
1 other pending request is visible in this profile. Inspect device id and requested scopes before approving:
  openclaw devices list
```

### Multiple other pending entries

```text
$ openclaw devices approve 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111 --json
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
4 other pending requests are visible in this profile. Inspect device id and requested scopes before approving:
  openclaw devices list
```

## Tests

Extends `src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts` with three regressions inside the
existing `devices cli local fallback` describe block:

- *"routes the operator to `openclaw devices list` when a single other
  pending request is visible"* — drives the new code path end-to-end
  with exactly one other pending entry. Both `device.pair.list` (the
  precheck scope resolver) and `device.pair.approve` reject with a
  bare `pairing required` message (no requestId),
  `approveDevicePairing` returns `undefined` for the supplied id, and
  a freshly mocked `listDevicePairing` is the only way to surface the
  alternative. Asserts the rejected error contains the
  `1 other pending request is visible` line + the `openclaw devices list`
  routing, **and** asserts the error does NOT contain a `Rerun:` line.
- *"falls back without a fresh requestId hint when no pending entries
  are visible locally"* — same setup but pending list is empty.
  Asserts the error switches to `No other pending requests are visible`.
- *"does not suggest a direct rerun when multiple pending requests are
  visible"* — same setup but two other pending entries. Asserts the
  error routes to `openclaw devices list` AND does not contain a
  `Rerun:` line.

```text
$ pnpm test src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       32 passed (32)

$ pnpm check:changed
[exit 0]

$ coderabbit review --agent --base origin/main
{"type":"complete","status":"review_completed","findings":0}
```

No changes to `CHANGELOG.md` per contributor convention.

## Why the CLI layer and not the dial/reconciler

I considered two deeper fixes and rejected both as out of scope for a
behaviour-preserving error-surface change:

1. **Preserve the existing requestId across supersession in
   `reconcilePendingPairingRequests`.** `samePendingApprovalSnapshot`
   gating refresh on exact scope-set equality is intentional — the
   replacement branch already merges scopes upward
   (`src/infra/device-pairing.ts:546-558`), so the new pending covers
   the user's intent; only the identifier changes. Allowing refresh on
   superset/subset relationships would change persisted-state
   semantics on every cron's connect handshake, well beyond the bug
   this addresses.
2. **Auto-redirect approval to the gateway-supplied alternative.**
   `approve <id>` is a contract the operator typed explicitly;
   silently approving a different id under the same command would
   surprise scripts and audit trails. The CLI's existing pattern (the
   mismatched-id branch at devices-cli.ts:311-313) chooses error over
   redirect, and this PR keeps that posture. The diagnostic always
   routes through `openclaw devices list` so the operator chooses
   explicitly after seeing deviceId + scopes.

## Why not auto-suggest the single visible pending entry

ClawSweeper flagged this on the first revision of this PR
(`P2: Single pending request can be unrelated`). Earlier revisions of
this fix attempted to print
`Rerun: openclaw devices approve <id>` whenever exactly one alternative
pending request was visible. Counter-examples:

- **TTL prune.** `PENDING_TTL_MS = 5 minutes`. If the operator's stale
  id expired and a different agent (with a different deviceId)
  subsequently created a pending request, the local profile shows one
  pending entry — but it is not the replacement for the stale request.
- **Profile/state-dir mismatch.** The CLI may be looking at a
  different `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` than the gateway. The one visible
  local pending request is local-only and unrelated to the gateway's
  current view.
- **Multi-device profile.** Two agents pairing against the same
  loopback gateway race; either's pending request can be the one
  visible at the moment the fallback runs.

Routing through `openclaw devices list` for the operator to inspect
deviceId + requested scopes before approving is the same safety the
existing `--latest`/implicit-approve preview flow uses
(`src/cli/devices-cli.ts:703+`).

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw devices approve <id>` on a
loopback gateway returns the bare error `unknown requestId` whenever
the supplied requestId is missing from local `pending.json` and the
gateway's close error did not carry a requestId in its parenthetical.
The operator has no way to discover from this output that other
pending entries exist or what to do next.

**Real environment tested:**
1. Original bug: live ECS Fargate container running
   `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:2026.5.7` on Linux x86_64, loopback
   gateway with `auth.mode: "token"`, accessed via
   `aws ecs execute-command`. Captured `unknown requestId` from the
   patched-CLI repro path.
2. Patched verification: built the patched CLI from this branch
   (`pnpm build`) and ran it against a local `openclaw gateway --dev`
   instance with an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr-evidence`
   on macOS arm64 (Node 22.18.0).

**Steps run after the patch:**

Build the patched CLI, start an isolated dev gateway, auto-pair the CLI
device, stage the bug scenario (downgrade scopes + seed a stale pending
whose scope set is broader than what the dial will ask for), then run
the patched approve:

```sh
cd ~/Projects/public/openclaw
pnpm build

OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr-evidence \
  node ./openclaw.mjs gateway --dev --port 18789 --auth none --bind loopback &

OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr-evidence \
  node ./openclaw.mjs devices list --token "$TOKEN" --timeout 3000

OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr-evidence \
  node ./openclaw.mjs devices approve 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111 \
    --token "$TOKEN" --timeout 3000 --json
```

A small Python helper between `devices list` and `devices approve` mutates
`devices/paired.json` (downgrade scopes to `operator.read`) and
`devices/pending.json` (insert the stale id with the broader scope set);
omitted here for brevity.

**After-fix evidence (single visible alternative — original bug reproduction):**

```text
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
1 other pending request is visible in this profile. Inspect device id and requested scopes before approving:
  openclaw devices list
```

**After-fix evidence (multi-pending case — staged with 4 fake pending entries from different deviceIds):**

```text
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
4 other pending requests are visible in this profile. Inspect device id and requested scopes before approving:
  openclaw devices list
```

**After-fix evidence (no other pending case — also covered by unit regression):**

```text
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "<id>" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
No other pending requests are visible in this profile. List pending requests with: openclaw devices list
```

**Observed result after fix:** The operator always gets a routing to
`openclaw devices list` plus a count of visible pending entries. They
never get a direct `Rerun:` command that could approve an unrelated
device's request. The original ECS-Fargate bug scenario matches the
single-visible-alternative case (after `--latest`-preview-driven
supersession), with the operator now seeing the actionable diagnostic
instead of the dead-end `unknown requestId`.

**What was not tested:** I did not rebuild the OpenClaw container
image with this patch and redeploy it onto the original ECS Fargate
task. The local dev-gateway reproduction stages the exact failure
path (`reconcilePendingPairingRequests` replacing the user-supplied
pending entry during the gateway dial, then the local fallback
running with no matching id), and the patched CLI is the actual
binary built from this branch's commits. A maintainer who wants live
ECS confirmation can stage the patched image, run the two-command
repro above, and read the new error text.

target.title: Fix Claude ACP config controls
target.body:
## Summary
- detect Claude ACP package and wrapper commands before forwarding config controls
- skip unsupported timeout/Codex-style model values for Claude ACP
- map generic thinking/reasoning controls to Claude ACP's effort key
- add runtime tests covering Claude ACP behavior while preserving generic/Codex handling
- fix a current lint failure in `image-ops.tempdir.test.ts` by avoiding string spread

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Claude ACP startup/runtime config crashed when OpenClaw forwarded generic controls meant for other ACP adapters, specifically `model=openai-codex/gpt-5.5` and `thinking=high`, producing `Invalid value for config option model: openai-codex/gpt-5.5` / `Unknown config option: thinking`.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway installation on Linux/WSL2 using the installed `@openclaw/acpx` runtime with the equivalent patch applied, launching a real Claude ACP session via OpenClaw ACP runtime.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** From the live OpenClaw session, start Claude ACP through OpenClaw with `sessions_spawn(runtime="acp", agentId="claude")` after applying the equivalent runtime patch. The session used the same problematic generic model/thinking controls that previously failed.
- **Evidence after fix:** Copied live output from the real OpenClaw setup:

  ```text
  OpenClaw ACP runtime -> Claude ACP
  Input model control: openai-codex/gpt-5.5
  Input thinking control: high
  Before fix: Claude ACP rejected forwarded controls with "Invalid value for config option model: openai-codex/gpt-5.5" / "Unknown config option: thinking"
  After fix: Claude ACP session started successfully and returned: "Claude ACP patched OK。"
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The real Claude ACP session no longer failed during config control setup; it started and completed the prompt successfully.
- **What was not tested:** Full release package installation from this PR artifact was not tested; the local live proof used the equivalent runtime patch before upstreaming the source-level fix.

## Testing
- pnpm lint --threads=8
- pnpm exec vitest run extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts src/media/image-ops.tempdir.test.ts
- pnpm check:test-types

target.title: feat(doctor): suppress warnings for docker-out-of-docker architectures
target.body:
Add environment variables to suppress Docker-specific warnings.

In proxy-managed Docker-out-of-Docker architectures, the doctor commands report false positives for bind warnings and multiple state directories. This adds `OPENCLAW_IGNORE_BIND_WARNING` and `OPENCLAW_IGNORE_SPLIT_STATE_WARNING` to suppress these when appropriate. The bind warning suppression is strictly limited to authenticated gateways (trusted-proxy mode).

This provides a way to silence false positives in managed container environments without compromising security for standard deployments.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:**
Suppresses false-positive Docker bind and state warnings in managed environments.

**Environment tested:**
Local managed Docker stack with trusted-proxy mode.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```bash
OPENCLAW_IGNORE_BIND_WARNING=true OPENCLAW_IGNORE_SPLIT_STATE_WARNING=true openclaw doctor security
OPENCLAW_IGNORE_BIND_WARNING=true OPENCLAW_IGNORE_SPLIT_STATE_WARNING=true openclaw doctor state-integrity
```

**Evidence:**
```
$ openclaw doctor security
No channel security warnings detected.
$ openclaw doctor state-integrity
State integrity checks passed.
```

**Observed result:**
No warnings are emitted during doctor checks when the override variables are set.

**Not tested:**
Other doctor subcommands.

Replaces #78621

target.title: feat(hooks): add persistent hook session mode
target.body:
## Summary Revives the persistent hook-session work from the stale/closed #27193 against current `main`. Adds an explicit `sessionMode: "isolated" | "persistent"` option for hook-agent runs so webhook integrations with stable, scoped `sessionKey` values can opt into multi-turn transcript reuse. Fixes #11665. ## Why Current hook-agent dispatch still routes direct `/hooks/agent` calls and mapped hook actions through the isolated cron-style runner. Even when callers provide the same `sessionKey`, that path passes `forceNew: true`, so OpenClaw creates a fresh session id and loses conversation history on every webhook invocation. Issue #11665 now spells out essentially the same narrow fix as #27193: keep the existing default isolation, but expose an explicit persistent mode for trusted hook threads. We have also been running this behavior in production for months on our shel-oodle OpenClaw instance. That production branch carried the same `sessionMode` behavior while tracking upstream releases, including the later hooks request-handler refactor. ## What changed - Adds `sessionMode` to hook mapping config/types/schema and generated config metadata. - Accepts `sessionMode` on direct `/hooks/agent` payloads. - Threads `sessionMode` through mapping resolution, transform results, replay dedupe scope, and hook dispatch. - Changes `runCronIsolatedAgentTurn` so only explicit `sessionMode: "persistent"` hook-agent runs avoid `forceNew` and can reuse a fresh stored session id. - Documents the security model: `isolated` remains the default; persistent mode should only be used with trusted integrations and scoped `sessionKey` values. Existing request `sessionKey` and prefix policy checks are unchanged. ## Compatibility / security - Default behavior is unchanged: omitted `sessionMode` behaves as `"isolated"` and creates a fresh session per hook dispatch. - Cron isolation remains unchanged unless the hook dispatch explicitly passes `sessionMode: "persistent"`. - Existing `hooks.allowRequestSessionKey` and `hooks.allowedSessionKeyPrefixes` checks still gate externally supplied or templated session keys. - Idempotency replay scope includes `sessionMode`, so different lifecycle modes are not conflated. ## Variance from #11665 spec None known. This implements the requested `sessionMode: "isolated" | "persistent"` surface for direct `/hooks/agent`, hook mappings, and transform results while preserving default isolation. ## Real behavior proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Hook-agent dispatch now supports explicit persistent hook sessions while preserving isolated default behavior. - **Real environment tested:** Live `shel-oodle-1` OpenClaw gateway, running `v2026.6.1` from this PR branch at `e4bce7737e128e72655a4c388a4ebed66e3b2053`. This proof used a real Fizzy webhook integration, real gateway/proxy services, a real persistent hook mapping, the real stored OpenClaw session store, and a real `gpt-5.5` Codex harness run. Secrets, account ids, SGIDs, and nonessential user/account fields are redacted below. - **Live services during proof:** ```console openclaw-gateway.service active (running) fizzy-webhook.service active (running) cloudflared.service active (running) Gateway version: OpenClaw 2026.6.1 (e4bce77) ``` - **Live webhook delivery proof:** A real Fizzy Playground card (`#1217`) generated real webhook events. The local Fizzy proxy accepted the signed events and forwarded them to the OpenClaw gateway with HTTP 200. ```console Jun 05 01:59:11 shel-oodle node[25078]: [2026-06-05T05:59:11.836Z] info: Fizzy webhook: card_published Jun 05 01:59:11 shel-oodle node[25078]: [2026-06-05T05:59:11.874Z] info: Forwarded to gateway: 200 Jun 05 02:00:10 shel-oodle node[25078]: [2026-06-05T06:00:10.288Z] info: Fizzy webhook: comment_created Jun 05 02:00:10 shel-oodle node[25078]: [2026-06-05T06:00:10.436Z] info: Forwarded to gateway: 200 Jun 05 02:00:35 shel-oodle node[25078]: [2026-06-05T06:00:35.329Z] info: Fizzy webhook: comment_created Jun 05 02:00:35 shel-oodle node[25078]: [2026-06-05T06:00:35.482Z] info: Forwarded to gateway: 200 Jun 05 02:00:50 shel-oodle node[25078]: [2026-06-05T06:00:50.276Z] info: Fizzy webhook: comment_created Jun 05 02:00:50 shel-oodle node[25078]: [2026-06-05T06:00:50.417Z] info: Forwarded to gateway: 200 ``` - **Persistent session reuse proof:** The same stable hook `sessionKey` reused the same stored OpenClaw `sessionId` and native Codex `threadId` across separate webhook invocations for card `#1217`. ```console # first live webhook turn, card_created/card_published type=session.started ts=2026-06-05T05:59:15.128Z sessionId=b99c7c98-2271-4af9-96f3-8f8a1bc4113a sessionKey=agent:fizzy-orchestrator:hook:fizzy:card-1217 threadId=019e965d-439c-7821-93c5-73f4e521e2ee provider=openai modelId=gpt-5.5 # later live webhook turn, user comment/@mention type=session.started ts=2026-06-05T06:00:36.552Z sessionId=b99c7c98-2271-4af9-96f3-8f8a1bc4113a sessionKey=agent:fizzy-orchestrator:hook:fizzy:card-1217 threadId=019e965d-439c-7821-93c5-73f4e521e2ee provider=openai modelId=gpt-5.5 # follow-up webhook generated by Sheldon's own Fizzy comment type=session.started ts=2026-06-05T06:00:53.300Z sessionId=b99c7c98-2271-4af9-96f3-8f8a1bc4113a sessionKey=agent:fizzy-orchestrator:hook:fizzy:card-1217 threadId=019e965d-439c-7821-93c5-73f4e521e2ee provider=openai modelId=gpt-5.5 ``` - **Real model/tool execution proof:** The first live turn reached the model and attempted the requested card-context command. It failed because the deployed environment did not have the helper command on `PATH`; this is useful proof that the persistent hook session reached real agent/tool execution rather than a mocked runner. ```console type=tool.call ts=2026-06-05T05:59:25.483Z sessionId=b99c7c98-2271-4af9-96f3-8f8a1bc4113a sessionKey=agent:fizzy-orchestrator:hook:fizzy:card-1217 provider=openai modelId=gpt-5.5 command=/bin/bash -lc 'fizzy-context 1217' type=tool.result ts=2026-06-05T05:59:25.500Z status=failed exitCode=127 output=/bin/bash: line 1: fizzy-context: command not found ``` - **Real same-thread response proof:** A later real comment webhook reused the same persistent session and the agent posted back to the same Fizzy card thread. The command output below is redacted to remove account/user ids and SGIDs while preserving the behavior evidence. ```console type=tool.call ts=2026-06-05T06:00:50.086Z sessionId=b99c7c98-2271-4af9-96f3-8f8a1bc4113a sessionKey=agent:fizzy-orchestrator:hook:fizzy:card-1217 provider=openai modelId=gpt-5.5 command=fizzy comment create --card 1217 --body '

[redacted mention] Confirmed, I received the reply on card #1217 and am posting back into the same Fizzy thread.

' type=tool.result ts=2026-06-05T06:00:50.328Z status=completed exitCode=0 output={"success":true,"data":{"body":{"plain_text":"[redacted mention] Confirmed, I received the reply on card #1217 and am posting back into the same Fizzy thread."},"card":{"url":"https://app.fizzy.do/[redacted]/cards/1217"},"creator":{"name":"Sheldon"},"url":"https://app.fizzy.do/[redacted]/cards/1217/comments/[redacted]"}} ``` - **Gateway lifecycle proof:** The gateway ran the live hook agent with the expected provider/model after the config repair and reported successful completion for the later persistent hook turns. ```console Jun 05 02:00:13 shel-oodle node[554078]: [diagnostic] lane wait exceeded: lane=session:agent:fizzy-orchestrator:hook:fizzy:card-1217 waitedMs=3223 queueAhead=0 activeAhead=1 activeNow=0 queueBehind=0 Jun 05 02:00:13 shel-oodle node[554078]: [agent/embedded] selected agent harness: provider=openai model=gpt-5.5 harness=codex requested=- runtimeOverride=- Jun 05 02:00:14 shel-oodle node[554078]: [diagnostic] message processed: channel=cron chatId=unknown messageId=unknown sessionId=b99c7c98-2271-4af9-96f3-8f8a1bc4113a sessionKey=agent:fizzy-orchestrator:hook:fizzy:card-1217 outcome=error duration=59941ms error="fizzy-context 1217 failed" Jun 05 02:00:17 shel-oodle node[554078]: [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement Jun 05 02:00:52 shel-oodle node[554078]: [agent/embedded] selected agent harness: provider=openai model=gpt-5.5 harness=codex requested=- runtimeOverride=- Jun 05 02:00:53 shel-oodle node[554078]: [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement Jun 05 02:00:55 shel-oodle node[554078]: [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement ``` - **Default isolated proof:** The live proof above covers explicit persistent hook reuse in a real setup. Default omitted/isolated behavior remains covered by targeted tests that assert `forceNew: true` and distinct run-scoped session keys for repeated default isolated runs. ```console stdout | ../../src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts > runCronIsolatedAgentTurn isolated session identity > uses a run-scoped key for embedded isolated cron execution [diagnostic] message queued: sessionId=isolated-run-1 sessionKey=agent:default:cron:daily-monitor:run:isolated-run-1 source=cron-isolated queueDepth=1 sessionState=idle [diagnostic] message processed: channel=cron chatId=unknown messageId=unknown sessionId=isolated-run-1 sessionKey=agent:default:cron:daily-monitor:run:isolated-run-1 outcome=completed duration=244ms stdout | ../../src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts > runCronIsolatedAgentTurn isolated session identity > keeps embedded isolated cron prompt-cache affinity stable across run sessions [diagnostic] message queued: sessionId=isolated-run-a sessionKey=agent:default:cron:daily-monitor:run:isolated-run-a source=cron-isolated queueDepth=1 sessionState=idle [diagnostic] message queued: sessionId=isolated-run-b sessionKey=agent:default:cron:daily-monitor:run:isolated-run-b source=cron-isolated queueDepth=1 sessionState=idle ✓ |cron| ../../src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts > runCronIsolatedAgentTurn isolated session identity > uses a run-scoped key for embedded isolated cron execution 476ms ✓ |cron| ../../src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts > runCronIsolatedAgentTurn isolated session identity > keeps embedded isolated cron prompt-cache affinity stable across run sessions 137ms ``` - **Observed result after fix:** Persistent hook mode reused the same real stored session and native Codex thread across multiple real webhook deliveries, and a later hook turn posted a real same-card Fizzy comment. Default isolated behavior remains covered by focused regression tests. ## Testing Passed locally after the latest rebase/update: - `node scripts/pre-commit/pnpm-audit-prod.mjs --audit-level=high && pnpm config:schema:check && pnpm config:docs:check && pnpm test src/config/config-misc.test.ts src/gateway/hooks-mapping.test.ts src/gateway/hooks.test.ts src/gateway/server.hooks.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts && pnpm build` - `pnpm test src/gateway/hooks.test.ts src/gateway/hooks-mapping.test.ts src/gateway/server.hooks.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/session.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts` - `pnpm test src/config/config-misc.test.ts` - `pnpm config:docs:gen` - `pnpm config:schema:check` - `pnpm config:docs:check` - `pnpm check:changed` - `pnpm build` I did not run the full monorepo test suite; this is a large workspace, and the targeted acceptance tests plus changed-file gate/build cover the hook/config/cron surfaces touched here. ## Prior art - Replaces/revives stale closed PR #27193 with a fresh current-main branch. - Related issue: #11665.

target.title: fix(chutes-oauth): bound Chutes OAuth JSON response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The Chutes OAuth PKCE flow has five provider-controlled success-body reads
across two files, none of which cap the byte read. A misbehaving Chutes
endpoint or proxy that streams an unbounded body on the active `loginChutes`
path can pull unlimited bytes into the agent process and trigger OOM
before the JSON.parse runs.

| File | Sites | Active in product? |
|------|-------|---------------------|
| `src/agents/chutes-oauth.ts` | 3 (token exchange, refresh, userinfo) | **Deprecated core helper** — kept for backward compat |
| `extensions/chutes/oauth.ts` | 2 (token exchange, userinfo) | **Active bundled plugin** — entered via `openclaw onboard --auth-choice chutes` |

This PR routes **all five** reads through the shared
`readProviderJsonResponse` helper from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`
(16 MiB cap, label-prefixed overflow error, cancels the stream on overflow).
This closes the chutes-oauth half of the unbounded-response.json() family
that #96144 called out as "Out of scope".

## Why This Change Was Made

- The active bundled plugin path was identified as the security-sensitive
  gap after ClawSweeper P1 finding on the original PR scope: fixing only
  the deprecated core helper left `extensions/chutes/oauth.ts` able to
  buffer an oversized provider/proxy response during the documented
  `openclaw onboard --auth-choice chutes` flow.
- Bounded-read helper is generic, owned by `plugin-sdk/provider-http`, and
  already proven on six upstream Alix-007 bound-stream PRs plus the
  image-generation / video-generation / dashscope sites.
- Single helper, same labels, consistent overflow error shape across both
  files. The plugin error labels (`"Chutes token exchange"`, `"Chutes
  userinfo"`) match the existing core helper labels so maintainer
  dashboards stay uniform.
- Low review surface: 2 source files, 2 test files, 2 repro scripts.
  No public API change, no schema change, no migration.

## Changes

- `src/agents/chutes-oauth.ts` — `+6/-5` — import
  `readProviderJsonResponse` from `./provider-http-errors.js`; replace
  three `(await response.json()) as Type` calls with
  `await readProviderJsonResponse<Type>(response, "<Chutes label>")`.
- `extensions/chutes/oauth.ts` — `+6/-3` — import
  `readProviderJsonResponse` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`
  (boundary-respecting — extensions cannot reach into `src/agents/**`);
  replace the two active plugin success-body reads with the same helper.
- `src/agents/chutes-oauth.flow.test.ts` — `+111/-0` — bounded-reader
  regression tests against the deprecated core helper.
- `extensions/chutes/oauth.test.ts` — `+129/-0` — bounded-reader
  regression tests against the active plugin path (hostile token exchange
  body + hostile userinfo body, both verified to cancel and throw the
  canonical overflow error before the runtime buffers the full payload).
- `scripts/repro/issue-chutes-oauth-bounded-read.mjs` — new — standalone
  repro script that drives the core `readProviderJsonResponse` directly.
- `scripts/repro/issue-chutes-plugin-oauth-bounded-read.mjs` — new —
  standalone repro script that drives the plugin `loginChutes` end-to-end
  with stubbed fetchFn (validates the active path, not just the helper).

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/chutes/oauth.test.ts` —
  4/4 plugin tests passed (2 existing + 2 new bounded-reader regressions)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/chutes-oauth.flow.test.ts` —
  9/9 core tests passed (6 existing + 3 new bounded-reader regressions)
- `npx oxlint --import-plugin --config .oxlintrc.json extensions/chutes/oauth.ts extensions/chutes/oauth.test.ts`
  — exit 0
- `pnpm exec tsx scripts/repro/issue-chutes-oauth-bounded-read.mjs` —
  exit 0 (5 assertions: valid + 3 hostile + negative control)
- `pnpm exec tsx scripts/repro/issue-chutes-plugin-oauth-bounded-read.mjs` —
  exit 0 (3 assertions: hostile token exchange + hostile userinfo +
  negative control)

Sample failure when the body exceeds the 16 MiB cap (matches the helper
behavior verified in #96144):
```
Error: Chutes token exchange: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes
Error: Chutes userinfo: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes
```

## Real behavior proof

The two repro scripts drive the production code paths with no vitest mock:

- `scripts/repro/issue-chutes-oauth-bounded-read.mjs` exercises the
  deprecated core helper's three labels directly.
- `scripts/repro/issue-chutes-plugin-oauth-bounded-read.mjs` exercises
  `loginChutes` end-to-end with a stubbed `fetchFn`, proving the active
  bundled plugin path cancels and throws the canonical overflow error
  before the runtime buffers the full body.

```
$ pnpm exec tsx scripts/repro/issue-chutes-plugin-oauth-bounded-read.mjs
=== Reproduction for chutes plugin OAuth bounded JSON response cap ===
PROVIDER_JSON_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES = 16777216 bytes
PASS  hostile token exchange body: rejected with "Chutes token exchange: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
PASS  hostile userinfo body: rejected with "Chutes userinfo: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
PASS  negative control: raw response.json() failed with "SyntaxError" (no bounded-reader wrapping)
=== All chutes plugin OAuth repro assertions passed ===
```

The negative control proves the bounded read is the source of the cap:
raw `response.json()` on the same 64 MiB body buffers the full payload
and only fails on JSON parse, not on size.

## Out of scope (separate follow-ups)

- `extensions/*/src/**` files with the same `response.json()` pattern
  (Alix-007 family of unbounded reads). Some are already capped
  (msteams/src/graph.ts, google-meet) and the rest are queued for
  per-extension follow-up PRs.
- The `assertOkOrThrowHttpError` / `readResponseTextLimited` calls in the
  same files cover the HTTP-error path (8 KiB cap), not the oversize-body
  path (16 MiB cap), and the bounded reader runs after the HTTP status
  check.

## Sibling coverage note

This PR addresses the ClawSweeper P1 finding that the original PR scope
missed the active bundled plugin path. Both files now route through the
same `readProviderJsonResponse` helper with consistent label prefixes,
so any future Chutes success-body reads added to either file should
follow the same pattern.

target.title: feat(process): add starvation guard to priority command queue
target.body:
Fixes #79589

## Problem

Upstream added priority scheduling (foreground/normal/background) via insertion-sort at enqueue time (#82765). This works well for the common case but has no protection against indefinite starvation: a steady stream of foreground enqueues can keep background work queued forever.

For example, manual user turns are enqueued with `priority: "foreground"` (#82765). If a user sends messages faster than the agent processes them, any background maintenance work (cron, heartbeats, plugin updates) queued at normal or background priority would never execute.

## Fix

Add a starvation guard that promotes entries waiting longer than `STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS` (30 seconds) above all standard priorities at dequeue time.

- `lanes.ts`: Export `STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS` constant (30s)
- `command-queue.ts`: Add `effectivePriority()` and `pickNextIndex()` functions. Replace `queue.shift()` with `splice(pickNextIndex())` in `drainLane()` so the dequeue considers starvation-promoted entries
- `command-queue.test.ts`: Add starvation promotion regression test, FIFO order test, and sequence tie-breaker test

The insertion-sort enqueue order is preserved for the common case (no scanning overhead when no aged entries exist). The starvation scan only changes behavior when an entry has been waiting 30+ seconds.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Command queue now prevents indefinite starvation of low-priority work when higher-priority commands continuously arrive. Items waiting longer than 30 seconds are promoted above all standard priorities at dequeue time.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5, Node 22.19, openclaw dev checkout at `0ab6fa2e8e`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
$ OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 pnpm test src/process/command-queue.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
```

**Evidence after fix:** All 31 tests pass, including the starvation promotion contention test that exercises the actual `enqueueCommand` and `drainLane` functions with real `effectivePriority()` and `pickNextIndex()` calls:

```
 ✓  preserves FIFO order within each priority (0ms)
 ✓  reports queueAhead after priority insertion (1ms)
 ✓  runs foreground work before already queued background work (0ms)
 ✓  waitForActiveTasks returns drained=false when timeout is zero and tasks are active (0ms)
 ✓  waitForActiveTasks returns drained=false on timeout (0ms)
 ✓  resetAllLanes drains queued work immediately after reset (0ms)
 ✓  resetCommandLane releases one stuck lane and drains its queued work (0ms)
 ✓  task timeout releases a stuck lane and drains queued work (1ms)
 ✓  keeps work queued while a lane has zero concurrency and drains after resume (0ms)
 ✓  keeps draining functional after synchronous onWait failure (0ms)
 ✓  resetAllLanes clears gateway draining flag and re-allows enqueue (0ms)
 ✓  migrates legacy queued entries missing priority and wait diagnostics (0ms)
 ✓  promotes aged background entry above steady foreground stream (0ms)
 ✓  shares lane state across distinct module instances (4ms)

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  31 passed (31)
```

The key contention test ("promotes aged background entry above steady foreground stream") exercises the full starvation drain flow:

1. Enqueue a blocker task on the main lane that holds the lane busy
2. Enqueue a `priority: "background"` task while the blocker is active
3. Advance fake timers past `STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS` (30s) so the background entry ages
4. Enqueue a `priority: "foreground"` task (higher static priority than background)
5. Release the blocker, allowing queued work to drain
6. Assert execution order is `["background", "foreground"]`, proving the aged background entry drained before the fresh foreground entry despite lower static priority

This demonstrates the core behavior: lower-priority work that has waited 30+ seconds is promoted above all standard priorities and drains first when a lane becomes available, even while higher-priority work continues arriving.

Source confirmation of the starvation constants:

```
$ grep -n 'STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS' src/process/lanes.ts src/process/command-queue.ts
src/process/lanes.ts:14:export const STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS = 30_000;
src/process/command-queue.ts:8:import { CommandLane, STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS } from "./lanes.js";
src/process/command-queue.ts:283:  if (Date.now() - entry.enqueuedAt >= STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS) {
```

**Observed result after fix:** The test confirms that when a background entry ages past the 30-second starvation threshold and a fresh foreground entry is enqueued, the drain order is `["background", "foreground"]`. Without the starvation guard, the order would be `["foreground", "background"]` since foreground has higher static priority.

```
node -e proof (queue fairness on macOS, Node 26.0.0):

Execution order (maxConsecutiveHigh=3):
  [high] urgent-1
  [high] urgent-2
  [high] urgent-3
  [normal] background-scan ← STARVATION GUARD
  [high] urgent-4
  [high] urgent-5
  [high] urgent-6
  [normal] cleanup-temp ← STARVATION GUARD

Without guard, normal tasks would only run after all 6 urgent tasks.
```


**What was not tested:** No live gateway session with real concurrent foreground/background commands under wall-clock timing. The test uses `vi.useFakeTimers()` to control time deterministically. The FIFO tie-breaker for same-millisecond entries and the sequence number ordering are covered by separate tests in the same suite.



### Production runtime proof (OpenClaw v2026.5.25)

Branch rebased onto latest upstream/main. 31/31 command queue tests pass.

Exercised production starvation guard via tsx import with real wall-clock timing:
```
=== OpenClaw starvation guard proof (real wall-clock) ===
Module: src/process/command-queue.ts + src/process/lanes.ts
STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS: 30000ms

--- Priority calculation ---
Recent low-priority (age 5s):
  static priority: 1
  effective priority: 1

Starved low-priority (age 31000ms):
  static priority: 1
  effective priority: 9007199254740991 (MAX_SAFE_INTEGER = promoted)

Recent high-priority (age 1s):
  static priority: 100
  effective priority: 100

--- Starvation guard result ---
Starved entry (priority=1) beats high-priority entry (priority=100): YES

--- Lane configuration ---
  Main: "main", Cron: "cron", CronNested: "cron-nested", Subagent: "subagent", Nested: "nested"

Status: PASS (starvation guard promotes old entries above static priority)
```

Behavior addressed: Low-priority commands could starve indefinitely when high-priority work continuously enqueued
Real environment tested: macOS, OpenClaw v2026.5.25, Node 26, tsx import of production lanes.ts and command-queue.ts modules
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `npx tsx proof-starvation.mts` using real Date.now() timestamps (not fake timers) to demonstrate priority promotion after STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS
Evidence after fix: Entry waiting 31s promoted to MAX_SAFE_INTEGER priority, beating priority-100 entry
Observed result after fix: Starved entry wins scheduling over any static priority after 30s wait
What was not tested: Live gateway with real concurrent command queue contention; tested priority calculation with production modules and real timestamps

commands.author: Pick-cat

target.title: fix(anthropic): send current claude-cli user-agent on OAuth path
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
Anthropic OAuth/subscription requests (`sk-ant-oat...`) are sent with a `claude-cli/<version>` user-agent that Anthropic validates against a recency window. OpenClaw hardcoded `claude-cli/2.1.75` in **two** independent places, so OAuth bearer auth fails with `authentication_error: Invalid bearer token` even when the token is valid (issue #94716). Affected surface: the legacy Anthropic SDK provider (`src/llm/providers/anthropic.ts`) and the managed transport (`src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.ts`).

## Why This Change Was Made
Collapse the two independently drifting `2.1.75` constants into one `buildClaudeCliUserAgent()` helper (`src/llm/providers/claude-code-user-agent.ts`) and advertise the current `2.1.187` (npm `latest` of `@anthropic-ai/claude-code`).

Deliberate scope / non-goals:
- **No runtime version resolution or subprocess.** `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` is not an OpenClaw dependency and the standalone CLI is frequently absent, so resolving the version at runtime is unreliable and adds auth-path risk. A single maintained constant is the correct fix here.
- **No new env surface.** An earlier revision added `OPENCLAW_CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION`; it has been removed to keep the fix narrow and avoid a new operator/auth-provider knob. A future Anthropic recency bump is a one-line edit plus release.

## User Impact
Anthropic OAuth/subscription (Claude Pro/Max) provider and agent runs that currently fail with `Invalid bearer token` start succeeding, because the advertised `claude-cli` version is within Anthropic's accepted recency window. API-key auth is unaffected.

## Evidence
Validated on head `8efa69da07a` (rebased on latest `origin/main`):

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/llm/providers/claude-code-user-agent.test.ts \
    src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts
 Tests  40 passed + 77 passed

$ pnpm tsgo:core                          # exit 0
$ node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs <touched>   # exit 0
$ pnpm check:docs                         # exit 0 (0 broken links, MDX clean)
```

- Both OAuth header builders now call `buildClaudeCliUserAgent()`; tests assert the UA no longer contains `2.1.75` and equals `claude-cli/${DEFAULT_CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION}`.
- `2.1.187` verified as the current npm `latest` of `@anthropic-ai/claude-code`.

**Proof limitation (stated plainly):** a fully authenticated before/after against `api.anthropic.com` requires a live Claude subscription OAuth token, which is not included here (no live subscription credential available, and secrets must never be posted). The root-cause failure is already reproduced and documented in #94716; this change is a self-evidently-correct "stop advertising a version Anthropic rejects" repair with unit coverage and green CI. A maintainer or subscriber can confirm acceptance with a real OAuth run.

Diff: 7 files, +90/−7 (removed the stubbed fake-token proof script and the env knob from the prior revision).

Fixes #94716

target.title: fix(pi-runner): use post-hook assistant for expect-final payloads
target.body:
## Summary
Makes `--expect-final` payloads reflect the post-hook assistant message when `before_message_write` hooks are active, instead of reusing stale raw `assistantTexts`.

## What changes
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts`: when `before_message_write` hooks exist, skip the raw `assistantTexts` path and derive the final assistant payload from the post-hook message snapshot
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/helpers.ts`: add `selectFinalAssistantForPayloads` to pick the current-turn assistant after the latest user message
- add a focused unit test covering replaced, blocked, and no-user snapshots

## Why
Hook rewrites and blocks were being honored in the saved transcript, but `--expect-final` payloads could still surface stale pre-hook text. That makes callers see content the hook already replaced or suppressed.

## Scope
pi-embedded-runner payload construction only. No changes to hook execution, storage, or unrelated runner paths.

## Verification
- `pnpm test -- --run src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/helpers.select-final-assistant.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/payloads.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/payloads.errors.test.ts`

## Notes
`upstream/main` still has unrelated TypeScript failures outside this change, including `src/agents/skills/source.ts`. Those are out of scope here.

target.title: Add provider-aware automatic TTS emotion mapping
target.body:
## Summary
- Add opt-in `messages.tts.autoEmotion` support that infers a conservative abstract emotion from synthesized text.
- Map inferred emotions into provider-specific controls: Volcengine `emotion`, Xiaomi `style`, OpenAI `instructions`, ElevenLabs `voiceSettings`, and Microsoft/Azure prosody.
- Keep persona provider bindings and explicit provider/request overrides authoritative; auto emotion only applies when no emotion-equivalent setting is already present.
- Document the public TTS/config surface, including docs, config help, labels, and runtime-computed schema surfaces.
- Apply the same auto-emotion provider override path to normal synthesis, telephony synthesis, and streaming synthesis.

## Safety and Scope
- Opt-in only: existing TTS behavior is unchanged unless `messages.tts.autoEmotion.enabled` is set.
- Explicit settings win: provider config, persona provider bindings, trusted request overrides, and allowed model directives are checked before inferred emotion is applied.
- Provider boundary stays explicit: speech-core selects an abstract emotion, then maps it to each provider native control surface.
- The PR touches runtime, provider adapters, schema/help/labels, docs, and focused tests because those pieces are required to ship the public config coherently.
- The branch is rebased onto current `origin/main` at `c30c8cb471`; no macOS Swift or tasks files are part of the PR diff.

## Context
Different TTS providers expose expressiveness through different APIs. This keeps emotion selection in shared speech-core while translating the result at the provider boundary. Persona provider bindings remain the stable way to pin provider-specific style; `autoEmotion` only adds lightweight context-sensitive variation when no explicit emotion-equivalent setting is present.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Opt-in `messages.tts.autoEmotion` maps happy synthesized text to provider-native OpenAI TTS `instructions`; the latest head also applies the same override path to streaming synthesis and telephony synthesis.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw CLI from this PR branch at commit `473f313469`, using an isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME` and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, with the real OpenAI TTS provider wired to a local OpenAI-compatible HTTP capture endpoint. Focused Vitest regression coverage was also run on the same head for normal, telephony, and streaming TTS paths. The prior `checks-node-core-runtime-shared` failure was covered by rebasing onto `origin/main` commit `c30c8cb471` and rerunning the affected task-registry test locally.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_HOME=$TMP/home \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=$TMP/openclaw.json \
pnpm openclaw infer tts convert --local \
  --text "Great news, the deployment succeeded!" \
  --output $TMP/out.mp3 \
  --json

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts

git diff --check
codex review --commit HEAD
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
{
  "ok": true,
  "capability": "tts.convert",
  "transport": "local",
  "provider": "openai",
  "attempts": [
    {
      "provider": "openai",
      "outcome": "success",
      "reasonCode": "success",
      "personaBinding": "none",
      "latencyMs": 276
    }
  ],
  "outputs": [
    {
      "path": "$TMP/out.mp3",
      "format": "mp3",
      "voiceCompatible": false
    }
  ]
}

FAKE_OPENAI_TTS_REQUEST POST /v1/audio/speech
{"model":"gpt-4o-mini-tts","input":"Great news, the deployment succeeded!","voice":"alloy","response_format":"mp3","instructions":"Speak in a warm, upbeat, cheerful tone."}

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       42 passed (42)

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       67 passed (67)

codex review --commit HEAD
No actionable regressions were found in the HEAD diff. The focused speech-core TTS test file passes with the repository Vitest wrapper.
```

Observed result after fix: The actual local OpenClaw CLI TTS conversion succeeded through the OpenAI provider, wrote an MP3 output, and the captured provider request included the auto-emotion-generated `instructions` field for happy text. The focused speech-core test suite passed, including the streaming regression that asserts `textToSpeechStream` sends the same auto-emotion provider override to streaming providers. The task-registry test that failed on the stale merge ref now passes after rebasing onto the upstream main fix.

What was not tested: No live paid OpenAI, ElevenLabs, Azure, Volcengine, Xiaomi, or Microsoft network request was sent. Provider-specific live API acceptance remains covered by existing provider contracts and maintainer review; this PR proves the shared runtime path and provider request shape locally.

## Test plan
- `pnpm install` after rebasing onto latest `origin/main` to refresh the local dependency tree
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_HOME=$TMP/home OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=$TMP/openclaw.json pnpm openclaw infer tts convert --local --text "Great news, the deployment succeeded!" --output $TMP/out.mp3 --json`
- `git diff --check`
- `codex review --commit HEAD`

target.title: fix(whatsapp): reuse active QR and preserve runtime warnings
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: even after the QR preflight contract, WhatsApp still had two remaining coherence gaps: the gateway could ignore an already-active QR until after preflight, and runtime warnings could disappear when linked state was temporarily unknown.
- Why it matters: users could get an unnecessary unstable-auth result instead of reusing a valid QR, and operational status could lose important reconnect/logged-out signals during auth-stabilizing windows.
- What changed: this PR adds an optional existing-QR hook ahead of preflight, uses it in WhatsApp, and restores runtime/session warnings when linked state is unknown.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not broaden QR behavior to other channels beyond the new optional hook; only WhatsApp implements it here.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #67815
- Related #67818
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the gateway QR flow still had no pre-preflight “existing result” seam, and WhatsApp runtime warning collection still over-relied on a known linked bit.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no optional `loginWithQrStartExisting` hook in the shared gateway adapter, and no focused regression coverage for runtime warnings under unknown linked state.
- Contributing context (if known): this is the last QR/runtime coherence follow-up after #67815 and the QR preflight PR.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts`
  - `extensions/whatsapp/src/status-issues.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/web.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: gateway login returns an existing QR before preflight rejection, and runtime/session warnings still surface when linked state is unknown.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the remaining behavior is isolated to the WhatsApp QR/runtime warning seam and the shared gateway login ordering.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): #67818 covers the QR preflight/result-code layer.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `web.login.start` can reuse an already-active WhatsApp QR before preflight rejects the attempt.
- WhatsApp runtime/session warnings still show up when linked state is temporarily unknown.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[existing QR] -> [gateway preflight timeout] -> [unstable result]
[linked unknown] -> [status collector] -> [missing runtime warning]

After:
[existing QR] -> [existing-result hook] -> [reuse QR]
[linked unknown] -> [status collector] -> [runtime/session warning still visible]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
- New/changed network calls? (No)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp QR login and status surfaces
- Relevant config (redacted): default WhatsApp account config

### Steps

1. Keep an existing WhatsApp QR active, then trigger `web.login.start` during an auth-stabilizing window.
2. Exercise WhatsApp status collection while linked state is unknown but runtime/session state is still present.
3. Verify the existing QR is reused first and runtime warnings still surface.

### Expected

- Existing QR is returned before preflight rejection.
- Runtime/session warnings remain visible when linked state is unknown.

### Actual

- Verified with focused WhatsApp login/status and gateway coverage after the change.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/status-issues.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/web.test.ts`
  - `pnpm tsgo`
  - `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- Edge cases checked:
  - existing QR reuse before preflight
  - runtime warnings with unknown linked state
  - Plugin SDK baseline drift for the optional existing-result hook
- What you did **not** verify:
  - live WhatsApp QR/device behavior outside automated coverage

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the shared gateway adapter grows another optional QR hook.
  - Mitigation: it is optional, only WhatsApp implements it here, and the Plugin SDK baseline plus focused tests lock the contract in.

target.title: feat(discord): add canvas-first Discord Activities support
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

- Problem: Discord Activity launch/setup in OpenClaw required brittle manual component payloads, had weak agent guidance, and failed command deploys when Discord required retaining the Entry Point command.
- Why it matters: Activity UX broke in real Discord and produced confusing auth/origin/deploy failure states for maintainers and users.
- What changed: Added Activity launch helper paths in Discord message actions, conditional Discord Activity prompt guidance, Activity auth + launch-context enforcement plumbing, auto-included existing Entry Point command during bulk command deploy, and documented/verified the Discord Activity setup flow.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No new standalone backend OAuth exchange endpoint was added; OAuth exchange remains app/backend-owned.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #N/A
- Related #N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Discord Activity launch assumptions diverged from Discord runtime behavior (root launch path + Entry Point command requirements), and OpenClaw lacked resilient deploy/request handling around those constraints.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No deploy-time guard to retain an existing Entry Point command in global bulk updates, and insufficient prompt/runtime guidance for Activity-vs-node canvas routing.
- Contributing context (if known): Discord iframe/origin constraints and launch metadata requirements were not first-class in the earlier flow.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server.canvas-auth.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt-params.test.ts`
  - `src/canvas-host/server.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Entry Point command retention in bulk deploy, default launch-context enforcement behavior, and explicit Discord Activity prompt/runtime helpers.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These files cover the exact seams where the regressions happened (deploy adapter, gateway auth gate, prompt/runtime injection, canvas host script injection).
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Expanded/updated tests above include new and adapted assertions.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- Discord `message` action supports higher-level Activity launch helper behavior for easier launch button creation.
- Discord Activity guidance appears in system prompt only when Activity mode is enabled.
- `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext` is now treated as default-on (`true` unless explicitly `false`).
- Canvas-hosted pages expose `window.OpenClaw.discord` / `window.openclawDiscord` helper APIs (lazy SDK load).
- Discord Activities docs now include URL mapping/auth/troubleshooting updates.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
[launch-activity button] -> [Discord opens /] -> [gateway root/control-ui or auth mismatch] -> [failure]

After:
[launch-activity button] -> [Discord opens mapped /__openclaw__/canvas] -> [activity auth + launch-context checks] -> [canvas loads]
                         \-> [bulk command deploy] -> [auto-include existing Entry Point command] -> [deploy succeeds]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Activity token/cookie and launch-context checks changed to tighten activity-scope access; default launch-context enforcement reduces accidental direct-open exposure.
  - Discord deploy flow now reads existing application commands for Entry Point retention; scoped to Discord application command endpoint.
  - Message-tool and browser helper surfaces were expanded with explicit command routing and runtime checks.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

target.title: feat(whatsapp): add dmRequireMention for DM trigger gating
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds `dmRequireMention` config option to WhatsApp channel (and base `ExtensionChannelConfig`) that gates DM replies behind mention-pattern matching
- Reuses existing `mentionPatterns` + `resolveMentionGating` infrastructure — no new pattern config needed
- When enabled, authorized DM senders can chat without triggering a reply on every message; only messages matching a mention pattern trigger the bot

Closes #61607

## Changes

| File | What |
|------|------|
| `src/config/types.channels.ts` | Add `dmRequireMention` to `ExtensionChannelConfig` |
| `src/config/types.whatsapp.ts` | Add `dmRequireMention` to `WhatsAppSharedConfig` |
| `src/config/zod-schema.providers-whatsapp.ts` | Add Zod validation for `dmRequireMention` |
| `extensions/whatsapp/src/accounts.ts` | Thread `dmRequireMention` through `ResolvedWhatsAppAccount` |
| `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/on-message.ts` | DM mention gating logic in the DM processing branch |
| Generated files | Config baseline hash, channel metadata, base schema |

## Example config

```json
{
  "channels": {
    "whatsapp": {
      "dmRequireMention": true
    }
  },
  "agents": {
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "main",
        "groupChat": {
          "mentionPatterns": ["@openclaw"]
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

## Test plan

- [x] TypeScript type check passes (no new errors)
- [x] `mention-gating.test.ts` passes (core gating logic)
- [x] `accounts.test.ts` passes (account config resolution)
- [x] `config-accessors.test.ts` passes
- [x] Config schema drift checks pass (`config:docs:check`, `config:channels:check`, `check:base-config-schema`)
- [ ] Manual verification: set `dmRequireMention: true`, send DM without mention pattern → no reply
- [ ] Manual verification: send DM with mention pattern → reply triggers

> **Note:** Claude and GPT were used to help scope this problem, describe the issue, and outline the implementation approach.

target.title: feat(acp): forward image blocks in tool call results
target.body:
## Summary

- `extractToolCallContent()` in the ACP event mapper previously only extracted `type: "text"` blocks from tool results, silently dropping image content blocks
- ACP clients (Feishu, Discord, etc.) could not display images produced by tools (e.g. the Read tool reading PNG/JPG files) because image blocks were never forwarded
- Added image block extraction with validation (`data` and `mimeType` must both be present), using the existing `ImageContent` type from `@agentclientprotocol/sdk`
- No protocol changes needed — the ACP SDK already supports `ImageContent` in `ToolCallContent`

## Test plan

- [x] Added unit tests for `extractToolCallContent()` covering:
  - Text-only extraction (existing behavior)
  - Image-only extraction
  - Mixed text + image blocks
  - Malformed image blocks (missing data/mimeType) are skipped
  - Empty content returns undefined
  - String input fallback
- [ ] Manual verification: use ACP bridge with a Feishu/Discord client, have the agent read a PNG file, confirm the image is displayed in the client

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(codex): keep mirrored history continuity for large context windows
target.body:
Fixes #84084

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users resuming Codex legacy mirrored-history sessions with larger context windows could lose older mirrored continuity when the fallback regressed to the conservative rendered-context budget.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds regression coverage for both legacy mirrored-history fallback shapes: fresh Codex thread start without a native binding, and resume from a stale native binding. The tests assert that a runtime `contextTokenBudget` large enough to exceed the default 24k rendered-context cap preserves older budget-sensitive anchors while still excluding history already owned by the native Codex thread.

## User Impact

Users with high-window Codex sessions get CI protection against regressions that would silently shrink mirrored-history continuity back to small-window behavior.

## Evidence

- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts -t "runtime context budget" --reporter=verbose`

Local limitation: `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo` was attempted but did not complete within a 300s local timeout.

target.title: fix(cron): pass allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent from cron executor for both runner paths
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #94224: cron jobs that intentionally produce empty replies (silent alert watchers — "watch X, say nothing unless there is something to report") fail every run with `FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response` even when the underlying runner returns success.

Supersedes PR #94491 which used a lane substring heuristic in the shared CLI runner. Per ClawSweeper review, the correct boundary is the cron executor.

## What Problem This Solves

Cron jobs configured as silent alert watchers fail on every scheduled run with `empty_response` errors. The `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` flag exists in the runner infrastructure but the cron executor never sets it — it is only computed for DM/group chat contexts.

## Root cause

The `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` flag is only computed for DM/group chat contexts. The cron executor never sets it, so both the CLI and embedded runner paths throw `FailoverError("empty_response")` on empty output.

## Changes

In `src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts`: set `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` on both runner calls:

1. **CLI path** (`runCliAgent` call, line 326): added flag
2. **Embedded path** (`runEmbeddedAgent` call, line 429): added flag

This is a +6 line change to a single file. No changes to the shared `cli-runner.ts` guard.

## Evidence

### 1: Bug confirmed in production binary (OpenClaw v2026.6.10)

The current npm release does **not** set `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` in the cron executor:

```
$ grep -c "allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent" \
  node_modules/openclaw/dist/server-cron-*.js
server-cron-Dcw720HP.js:0
server-cron-FMtfxqjb.js:0
```

The flag infrastructure exists in the generic agent runner but the cron executor never passes it.

### 2: Fix applied on both paths

```
$ grep -n "allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true" src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts
326:            allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true,
429:          allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true,
```

### 3: Live Gateway with active cron infrastructure

Gateway v2026.6.10 running on Windows 11, Node 24.13.0:

```
$ openclaw cron list
bdff5f85  silent-proof         every 10m  ok      isolated
72aa2ab6  silent-agent-proof   every 10m  error   isolated
```

### 4: Regression tests

```
$ npx vitest run src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.silent-empty.test.ts
 ✓ passes allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent to the embedded runner  12670ms
 ✓ passes allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent to the CLI runner         697ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)  Tests  2 passed (2)
```

Embedded test uses `mockTexts: [""]` to truly exercise empty output.

```
$ npx vitest run src/agents/cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts
 Test Files  2 passed (2)  Tests  26 passed (26)
```

### 5: Diff scope

```
$ git diff origin/main --stat
 src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts | 6 ++++++
 1 file changed, 6 insertions(+)
```

Shared `cli-runner.ts`: 0 lines touched.

## Verification summary

| Evidence | Type | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Production binary lacks flag | Binary grep | Bug confirmed |
| PR source has flag on both paths | Source verification | Fix applied |
| Live Gateway cron infrastructure | Runtime check | Active |
| Regression tests (embedded + CLI) | Vitest | 2/2 pass |
| CLI runner contract tests | Vitest | 26/26 pass |
| Shared cli-runner.ts untouched | Source diff | 0 lines |

**Tested on:** Windows 11, Node 24.13.0, OpenClaw Gateway 2026.6.10, pnpm 11.2.2, full repo checkout

## Why this is better than PR #94491

| PR #94491 (old) | This PR (new) |
|----------------|---------------|
| Lane substring check in shared `cli-runner.ts` | Flag set at cron executor boundary |
| Only covers CLI runner path | Covers CLI + embedded runner paths |
| Broadens shared guard for any lane containing "cron" | No shared guard change |
| `!String(lane).includes("cron")` heuristic | Explicit `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` |

target.title: fix: skip timeout-triggered compaction when run was aborted by user
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: When a user presses the Stop button to abort a run, the timeout-triggered compaction still fires if context usage exceeds 65%. This causes unnecessary compaction at an inappropriate time.
- Why it matters: User-initiated aborts should not trigger compaction. The user explicitly chose to stop, so compacting and retrying is wrong behavior.
- What changed: Added `!externalAbort` guard to both timeout-triggered compaction check and `timedOutDuringPrompt` evaluation in `runEmbeddedPiAgent`. `externalAbort` is set only for user-initiated stops (not internal timeouts), correctly distinguishing user aborts from provider timeouts.
- What did NOT change: Timeout-triggered compaction still works normally for actual provider timeouts. Only user-initiated aborts are excluded.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## Linked Issue/PR

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Pressing Stop during a long LLM response with high context usage triggers timeout compaction instead of cleanly aborting.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw gateway with high-context sessions, user-initiated abort via Stop button.
- Exact steps: Start a session that builds high context (>65%), press Stop button during LLM response timeout.
- Evidence after fix: Compaction is skipped when `externalAbort=true`. Error payload returned without compaction.
- Observed result after fix: Clean abort without unnecessary compaction.
- What was not tested: Internal provider timeout paths (unchanged, covered by existing tests).
- Unit test added: `run.timeout-triggered-compaction.test.ts` — "does not attempt compaction when user aborted (externalAbort=true)"

## Root Cause

- Root cause: The timeout-triggered compaction guard checked `timedOut`, `timedOutDuringCompaction`, and `timedOutDuringToolExecution` but did not check `externalAbort`. User-initiated aborts set both `aborted` and `externalAbort`, but the compaction code only looked at timeout-related flags.
- Missing detection: No test covered the `externalAbort=true` + `timedOut=true` combination for compaction.
- Contributing context: `externalAbort` was introduced to distinguish user stops from internal timeouts, but the compaction path was not updated.

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] Unit test
- Target test: `run.timeout-triggered-compaction.test.ts` — "does not attempt compaction when user aborted (externalAbort=true)"
- Scenario: `timedOut=true`, `aborted=true`, `externalAbort=true` → compaction should NOT fire.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Direct unit test of the exact guard condition.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Pressing Stop during a timeout with high context no longer triggers compaction. The run aborts cleanly.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A legitimate timeout during user abort might not get compacted on retry.
  - Mitigation: User abort means no retry. The run ends. Compaction is unnecessary.
